---
title: "On Scientific Writing and Blogs"
author: "Peter Raidl"
date: "2024-09-14"
categories: [news, blog, science, collaboration]
image: "Freud_Hysterie.png"
draft: True
---

## Scientific Writing

The first scientific journal was installed by the Royal Society in 1665 (see the [Philosophical Transactions on JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/journal/philtran1665167). Today, publications <!--#in peer-reviewed journals--> are the most valuable currency in science. I guess it is with good reason. Publishers made it possible for the community to distribute knowledge. The speed at which it was and is distributed is rather slow and there are different reasons for that. Nevertheless, progress in the sciences around the world would have been way slower without those printed journals.

Today, the big publishing houses get some bad press [@editorialGuardianViewAcademic2019]. Mostly, because publishers make a lot of money without creating the product. It sounds like a scam if you hear this for the first time. Traditionally, readers pay for the article they want to read. This means that many universities and research facilities pay a yearly fee to allow all their employees and students access to scientific journals.<!--# == WHAT DOES A UNI PAY for journal subscriptions???==-->

## How does the publication system work?

The journal's role is distribution of the knowledge and achievements made worldwide. The only way to make research count is to publicize it. In theory this could be any way of writing it down thoroughly and providing access to the rational, hypothesis, results and data to the community. Practically, the only way to make it count as an academic achievement is the publication in a scientific journal. There are some other ways like books, contributions at a conference, patents or preprints, but they are rare and sometimes considered less prestigious. And from a personal level, what counts at the end of the day is how prestigious your publication list is.

Researchers hand in the manuscript of their work and hope that it gets accepted by the journal and swiftly published. Of course, journals would like to keep some standards and check the quality of the manuscript. First, the journal editor decides if the manuscript is worth a more thorough review process altogether. Editors have a key role in separating the weed from the chaff. I can only imagine how much work it is to go through all the manuscripts handed in. Criteria for desk rejection could be that the topic does not fit the journal or obvious scientific shortcomings that can not be fixed. <!--# Editor Miyakawa, 2020. --> As there are a lot of journals <!--#(==Check how many listed==)-->, many try to have some unique selling points. These, again, guide the editors decisions.Common guidelines are that the paper should provide novel ideas or insights. Which means that repeating the same or similar experiments is hard to publish as are real replication attempts. This is not true for all fields. We recently so that in physics with (another) seemingly fraudulent attempt to create superconductors. Labs around the world tried to replicate the authors experiments without success. People seem to debate if that many replications were necessary in that particular case. It seemed unlikely to begin with. Some red flags already indicated that something was wrong. Nevertheless, Replications are and important part of science. They are the equivalent of measure twice, cut once. But replications don't sell. So many journals do not like to accept them. The editor has to make sure that the journal lives by its standards and sell. In the background, the publisher want's as many papers to be published in their journal as possible. Again, there are literary no costs involved to do so. So depending on the journals agenda, the editor might get some economical outlines on what to accept, too. You could only hope that the editor is well paid by the publisher, right? In some cases, editors might get some compensation. But in many reputative journals, editors are academics getting paid by the university and playing editor in their spare time.

Lets say that the editor accepts a manuscript judging it worthy a more thorough inspection. Not so long ago, \[Nature\](<https://www-nature-com.uaccess.univie.ac.at/>) was the first journal to install systematic peer-review for all handed in manuscripts(==SOURCE on PEER REVIEW==).

-   The idea behind peer-review

-   Famous story of Einstein not wanted peer-review

-   No-one gets paid

The editor of the journal decides if the article is worthy deeper inspection by the reviewers or alternatively desk-rejects the article. Rejections are made mostly based on the general idea behind the paper. Does it

Their products (papers) are written by the scientists who are mostly payed by universities (mostly taxes). The editors of the journals and the peers who review the papers are also volunteers from within academia. They do not earn any extra credits for this work. Especially the peer review is entirely out of good-will as they are mostly anonymous. Some journals keep a list of all peers which is publicly available. But nobody cares about that. When the peer-review is completed and the paper is ready for publication, the real fraud starts. Publishing itself often costs money. If you want everybody in the world being able to access your paper, publication might cost your institute 10.000\\\$ or more (Nature, access [@APCCellJournal; @PublishingOptionsNature]! On the other hand, the more traditional way is to let the reader pay instead of the authors. Universities around the world pay horrendous amounts of money to be able to access articles that their employees write! In some cases both parties pay - the readers and the authors. To be fair, things are improving. Open access journals getting more popular. In this system the authors pay once, which makes science at least more access able. On the other hand, this system does privilage insitutes with more money, while scientists in less economically strong areas of the world have a hard time to publish findings. There are some Diamand open access journals, meaning that nobody pays anything. This is what most scholars would prefer\
The academic community seems to be frustrated with the current situation. Nearly every scientist I know regularly drops complaints or self-aware jokes about the current system. There is a lot of discussion online and everybody seems to know what *Elsevier*, *Springer* and *Wiley*, the universities or funding agencies should do, to make the world a better place. The situation reminds me of each soccer world-cup during which everyone becomes a sports analyst and coach, knowing exactly what to do.

Today I want to approach the problem from another perspective. What can scientists do to improve the situation?\
Maybe not much. Because your publication list *is* what makes you a successful scientist. I doubt that the system will change overnight. Still, we should at least attempt to have a positive influence on the situation. Being patient is a great value with surprising research results and so it is with complex systems like science itself.

## Double Commitment - Life Outside the Ivory Tower

As I have written on the front page, science, and scholarship are activities of leisure. But scientists have to live on something, too. Many Western scientists of the past have lived from family wealth. They did the experiments and observations out of curiosity, leisure, fame, glory or to serve god. Nowadays, things are different. While academic researchers do enjoy prestige in some regards, the money that can be made is relatively limited. This is especially true concerning extensive education, high working hours, and the low probability of reaching the top of the food chain, which mostly means getting tenure. An alternative route for many is going into industry. This is a more realistic route for some research areas than others and nearly impossible for some.

![academia vs industry](academia_industry.jpg){width="250"}

Sometimes, there is some middle ground. Some scientists hit the "jackpot" and do both. They enjoy the freedom of academia and, as a related side-show, they work in the private sector. For health and performance-related fields (the ones that I am most interested in) like sports- and exercise science, psychology, and nutrition, this might mean giving seminars, selling books, working with supplement companies, or leveraging one's research idea into other profitable products.

This is more common than one might think. Probably, some big names come to your mind that do exactly that. So, in essence, they are selling the expertise they have carved out over years or decades of work. Fair thing, right? Let me make this clear: Some of independent scientists directly use the leverage they gain to fund their research with good intentions. Some claim that their breakthroughs would have not been possible in the *traditional way* because nobody believed in their work and they couldn't get funding.

Researchers are faulty human beings. No matter if they are funded by a national funding agency, by their personal company, or by personal beliefs and the urge to be right. We are invested in our ideas and hypothesis. I do not know if **everybody** iss, but I certainly am[^1]. I do not believe that being invested in an idea is completely avoidable. But caution is advisable if scientists sell products (or knowledge) related to their research without doing that directly as or in their role of the research institution. It might sound like a good thing if popular Stanford professors state that their podcast is independent of their role as a researcher, but I am not convinced that can be true. Not only because they (probably) have no chance but being the same person!

[^1]: and if there is one thing I learned from stats, it's that, most likely, I am a typical person and others think and act similarly.

Sure, "*research is me-search" -* and personal investment might come with higher motivation, drive and creativity. This makes the objective work vulnerable and contaminates your role as a researcher. Another profession knows about that for hundreds and thousands of years. So, in many traditions, priests are not allowed to have a family or sex. That's how they stay independent and hard to bribe (in theory).

## But Why is Selling Books Bad?

Making research available to the public is a significant component of modern science and is incentivized and with good reason. We want educated and responsible citizens!

**BUT WHY SELL IT???** That's the whole point.

We, want people - all people - to get access to the knowledge base. We are unhappy with the power of big publishing houses. But, it is hard to change that in the case of peer-reviewed articles without risking one's career. We talk about the [FAIR](https://www.go-fair.org/fair-principles/) principles within the ivory tower of science. But why not expand these principles beyond that?

We can and should leverage all those problems into **unrestricted publications** via **blogs** or **free internet books**.

**Publish your teaching openly:** I myself started putting one of my undergraduate seminars into writing and publishing it on Bookdown[^2]. This makes my seminar not only more transparent for students but also allows the information to be updated on a regular basis. Even after finishing the course, students can easily access the information. One could argue that you no longer need to go to the seminar as all the information is accessible for free on the Internet. But in my opinion, that is the case anyway. I can not tell our students any exercise training secrets anyway. Nobody should go to university for that explicit, teachable information anymore. We have passed that point a long time ago! The good news is that we can focus on experimental learning and carve out competencies[^3]!

[^2]: It is in German and addresses bachelor sports and human movement science students. It is work growing and work in progress. But you can find it [here](https://bookdown.org/peter_raidl/personalcoaching_script) if you are interested in exercise related Personal Coaching.

[^3]: Higher levels of Bloom's taxonomy of knowledge: apply, analyze, evaluate, and create [@krathwohlRevisionBloomTaxonomy2002]

**Writing blog posts about your research:** Traditional scientific journal articles can only withstand so much information. The general consensus in many fields is not to spoil the reader with too much personal experience, different challenges during different phases of the project. I think even in pre-prints and other publishing formats that are more researcher centered, we hold on to the traditional writing style like using passive voice as if everything was executed by a emotionless and error-free research-robot. In reality there is often some kind of thinking, tweaking, rethinking and frustration involved. Sharing those things might improve project science. Writing those protocols in a personal way might not only help yourself resolving some of the project related issues but also make the work of the grad-student working on a similar project at another university easier. *Humanizing* our daily work by writing about it, is also a way to make science and scientists more relate-able for people outside of academia. We do have this problem of trust towards science and just informing people "how reality works" does not do the trick to improve that, in my opinion. But being relate-able is also something we should work for within *Team Science*. Seeing that all science (not only my project) is messy. There are struggles that go beyond low statistical power and deadlines. I personally would love to see more unrestricted, writing from scientists. I would love to see less profit oriented side-gigs and more real content - raw, personal and messy as it is and I hope you do too. Because that is what I will be trying with this blog.
